speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number six. Hilde Scheraga is the survivor being interviewed. Mrs. Scheraga, will you continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to introduce the person on my right, who happens to be my husband, Morton G Scheraga. And I think he would like to say a few words.

speaker: interviewee
text: As Hilde may have told you, we are both active in the Holocaust child survivor support groups here in Connecticut, she as the hidden child. and I as a spouse am privileged to sit in on those support group meetings and participate in them. One of the incredible things that I have observed as an American growing up with a relatively normal childhood here in America is that these people, almost if I have to generalize, have been able to rebuild their lives after these horrible experiences they had of losing their childhoods. Most of them are professionals-- doctors, lawyers, successful business people. And if I have to generalize, it's one of the miracles of the Holocaust to me that they have been able to rebuild their lives from the ashes of the Holocaust. And if there's any embodiment of that courage and human spirit to go on with their lives, I see it in my wife, Hilde. After leaving Belgium at the age of 16, as you may have heard, she came to America not speaking any English at all. And within two years, graduated from high school, went on to work at the UN, marry, raise a family. And while she was raising a family, was able to finish her bachelor's degree and then go on to two master's degrees and a career in teaching. And at the height of her career, and even at the height of her husband's very successful career, she faced another devastating blow in her life-- the loss of her husband at the relatively young age of 46 when she was widowed and a single parent for six years until we met. Here again, she showed tremendous courage and spirit when she, after we were married, she gave up a beautiful home in Toronto, Canada, left a community that she had known for 20 years where she had made many friends, gave up her career in teaching, and once again became a stranger in a new land here in America, in Stanford, Connecticut. Rather than go back to teaching, she became the consummate volunteer here in Connecticut. She volunteered much of her time at the Video Archive Group at Yale University, doing much the same work as being done here, recording these testimonies. And at the same time, joined Jewish Family Service in their Senior Companion Program. But shortly after she worked with Jewish Family Service, they reached out, tapped her to develop the Russian resettlement program in preparation for the onslaught of many Russians who were about to come to this part of the country. Without any experience in managing or knowing how to develop such a program, she took on the responsibility and did a tremendous job in helping to settle here in Stanford, Connecticut hundreds of Russians. She became the Pied Piper of the community, attracting a whole corps of volunteer families who helped her find apartments, find furniture, raise funds, teach these immigrants English, help them find jobs. It was a total effort on her part, seven days a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was not unusual for Hilde to get calls at midnight, which she encouraged. Hilde, my child is sick, has 105 fever. What should I do? She became the mother to all of these Russian families. In fact, a few years after the program was underway, they gave her a voluminous book, in which each of them had written testimonials of thank you to her. And the frontispiece has a picture of the Statue of Liberty, on which they superimposed Hilde's face instead of the face of the statue. That's what Hilde Scheraga meant to this community and the Russians that she helped resettle.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a result of that work, she, as you may have heard, she was recognized as the outstanding community leader in the city of Stanford, not only in the Jewish community, but the entire community of Stanford, Connecticut. Soon after, they tapped her again. They kicked her upstairs to become the president of Jewish Family Service. And here again, she helped take a small agency and build it up to the point where it is now, one of the three largest agencies here in Stanford, Connecticut. And so you can see how proud I am of my wife. And I thank her. And I'm grateful to her for 13 beautiful years of our wonderful marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I'd just like to add one thing that really goes back that Mort reminded me of when he said I left my home in Toronto. Material things-- oh, I love them. And I enjoy all the comforts that I can get. But they have never been so important that I can't give them up. I had no problems getting rid of my house.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no regrets about selling my house. I had regrets about leaving so many friends behind. But things are-- people said, how can you sell your house? Well, to me, a house is a place where you live. And it doesn't really matter where the house is as long as you lead a good life. And giving up a relationship for the sake of the house, I just could never-- could never understand that. I-- I just thought [LAUGHS] that it's another thought that perhaps only survivors think of. I may be wrong. Anyway, thank you. Thank you both very much.
